lines.text: Section three. Open online databases.
Genome sequences are only useful if people can actually access them.
A single bacterial genome contains millions of base pairs of DNA; a human genome contains around three billion.
These are not data sets that can be disseminated on paper or shared via mailed CDs.
What made these data sets usable was the rise of the World Wide Web as a scientific medium, in the 1990s and especially the 2000s.
Today, the typical workflow when you want to know about an organism's gene is: open a web browser, query an online database, get back a richly annotated sequence and a great deal of supporting data.
Several specific databases have become indispensable.
The Saccharomyces Genome Database, often abbreviated SGD, hosts the entire 12-million-base-pair yeast genome with annotations of over 6,000 genes and links to thousands of experimental studies.
FlyBase serves the Drosophila research community in the same way.
There are equivalent databases for E. coli, for human, for mouse, and for every model organism worth studying.
These databases also serve a second function beyond data hosting: they become focal points for entire research communities.
An open database with thousands of users effectively becomes the lingua franca of the field.
And many of them — particularly the modern generation — operate on a wiki model, where researchers themselves can input and edit information and benefit from a constantly-improving shared resource.
Synthetic biology has built its own online databases on top of this foundation.

OpenWetWare.org is a wiki where researchers share experimental protocols — the kind of detailed lab procedures that are critical for reproducibility but rarely make it into formal publications in full detail.
parts.igem.org — the iGEM parts registry — is the canonical synthetic biology parts database, hosting thousands of biological parts contributed by iGEM competition teams.

Beyond synthetic biology's own databases, the field also makes heavy use of databases describing chassis cells (so that engineers can understand what they are modifying), databases listing enzymes and metabolic pathways across all of biology (so that engineers can recruit useful genes from anywhere in nature), and databases of regulatory motifs and protein structures.
The shift from data-as-physical-resource to data-as-online-resource is one of the quiet but enormous changes that distinguishes modern biology from the biology of even thirty years ago.
And synthetic biology was born into a world where this shift had already happened.
It is one of the field's gifts.
